title: Context Windows
lesson:
A context window is the maximum amount of text a model can pay attention to at once — its working memory. It is measured in Tokens (the small sub-word chunks a model actually reads), not in words or characters.

Everything the model can "see" while answering you must fit inside this one window:

  • your question
  • the system instructions
  • any documents you pasted
  • the reply it is currently writing

Go over the limit and something has to give.

The whiteboard analogy

Picture a whiteboard of a fixed size. You can write the problem, your notes, and the answer on it — but only so much fits. When the board is full and you need more space, you must erase something old to make room. A model's context window is exactly this whiteboard.

If a model has a 128k context window, that means roughly 128,000 tokens of board space, shared between everything that goes in and everything that comes out. As of 2026, common windows range from around 8k on small local models to 200k, 1M, and even 2M tokens on the biggest hosted ones. (1M tokens is roughly 750,000 words — about ten thick novels.)

It is not long-term memory

This trips up almost every beginner. A model does not remember your last conversation. Each time you call it, you hand it a fresh, blank whiteboard and write everything it needs to know, right then.

A chatbot "remembers" earlier messages only because the app quietly re-sends the whole conversation every single turn. Think of a goldfish with a notepad: the fish forgets instantly, but a helper keeps re-reading the notepad aloud before each question. Once the notepad fills up, the oldest notes get dropped — and the model genuinely cannot see them anymore.

The window is split between input and output

Here is the part people forget when they hit errors. The window holds both what you send (the prompt, your input) and what the model generates (its reply). If your context window is 8,000 tokens and your prompt already uses 7,800, the model has only ~200 tokens left to answer with — it will get cut off mid-sentence. This is why providers often expose a separate max_tokens setting: it reserves room on the whiteboard for the reply.

window      = 8192   # total tokens the model can hold
prompt_used = 7800   # your instructions + docs + chat history
reply_room  = window - prompt_used   # only 392 tokens left to answer!
print(reply_room)

Why not just make windows infinite?

Two hard costs stand in the way:

  • Compute. The Attention Mechanism (the part of a Transformer that lets every token look at every other token) traditionally scales quadratically — double the tokens and the work roughly quadruples.
  • Memory. The model stores a running cache of past tokens (the KV Cache) in GPU memory, and that cache grows with every token in the window. A huge context can eat more VRAM than the model's own weights.

So bigger windows mean slower, pricier, more memory-hungry calls. Big context is real, but it is never free.

⚠️ "Lost in the middle": models often use the very start and very end of a long context well, but skim what's buried in the middle. A fact on page 200 of a 300-page paste can be quietly ignored. A big window is permission to include more — not a promise the model reads it all carefully.

Where this bites in real products

Say you build a support bot over a 500-page manual. You cannot paste the whole manual into every question — it would overflow even a 1M window, cost a fortune, and bury the answer in the middle. Two standard fixes:

  • Retrieval. Use RAG: instead of dumping everything, fetch only the handful of relevant passages and place those in the window.
  • Summarizing old history. Once a conversation gets long, compress the first twenty messages into one paragraph so the recent, important turns still fit.

Both tricks share one goal: spend your limited whiteboard space wisely.

Context windowTraining memory
Temporary working memory, reset every callKnowledge baked into the model's weights, permanent
You fill it fresh each requestLearned once during training, never changes at use-time
Holds your specific docs and chatHolds general world knowledge from training data

Keep these two separate in your head. When you "teach" a model facts by pasting them into the prompt, you are using the context window — that knowledge vanishes the moment the call ends. To change what the model knows permanently, you'd need Fine-Tuning instead.
mentalModel: The context window is a whiteboard of fixed size that you wipe clean and rewrite on every single call. The model only ever sees what's currently on the board — your prompt and its reply share that space, and nothing carries over once the call ends.
takeaways: It's the model's working memory, measured in tokens — not words.
Your prompt and the reply share one fixed budget every call.
It resets each request — it is not long-term memory.
mistakes: Believing the model "remembers" past chats. It doesn't. Your app re-sends the whole conversation each turn. Once that history overflows the window, the oldest messages are dropped and the model truly cannot see them.
Forgetting the reply needs room too. The window is shared by input and output. Fill it almost full with your prompt and the answer gets chopped off mid-sentence. Always reserve space with max_tokens.

Assuming a bigger window means the model reads everything carefully. Thanks to the "lost in the middle" effect, facts buried in the center of a long context are often skimmed or ignored. Put the critical bits near the start or end.

Measuring the window in words or characters. It's counted in tokens. A 100k-character document is not 100k tokens — and code, numbers, and non-English text are far less token-efficient, so they fill the window faster.

Treating a huge context as a substitute for retrieval. Pasting a 500-page manual into every call is slow, expensive, and unreliable. RAG — fetching only the relevant pieces — almost always beats brute-force pasting.
Confusing context with permanent knowledge. Stuffing facts into the prompt is temporary and gone after the call. To change what a model knows for good, you need fine-tuning, not a longer prompt.

diagram.caption: A single fixed token budget holds the prompt and the reply together, and it is wiped and re-filled on every call.

exercise:
Take a model you can call (or any online token counter) and find its advertised context window — say 128k tokens. Now grab a real document, like a long article or a chapter of a PDF, and count its tokens. Work out three numbers: (1) how many copies of that document would fit in the window, (2) how many tokens are left for a reply if you paste in one copy, and (3) roughly how many words 128k tokens is (use ~0.75 words per token). Write down each answer.

Stretch goal: Build a tiny "context budget" function in code: given the window size, the tokens used by your system prompt, the tokens in your retrieved documents, and a desired reply length, return whether the request fits — and if not, how many tokens you must trim. Test it with numbers that just barely overflow.
solution: For 128k tokens: (3) is the easy one — 128,000 × 0.75 ≈ 96,000 words, roughly a 350-page book. If your article is, say, 8,000 tokens, then (1) about 16 copies fit (128,000 ÷ 8,000), and (2) after one copy you have ~120,000 tokens left for the reply (minus any system prompt). For the stretch goal, the core is a single subtraction: free = window - (system + docs + reply). If free is negative, that's exactly how many tokens you must cut — usually by trimming or summarizing the retrieved docs. The key insight: every part competes for the same fixed budget, and the reply must be reserved before you fill the rest.
summary: A context window is a model's working memory: the maximum tokens it can attend to in one call, shared between your prompt and its reply. It is not long-term memory — it resets every request, so chat apps re-send history each turn. Bigger windows cost more compute and memory and don't guarantee careful reading (the "lost in the middle" effect). For large knowledge, prefer RAG over pasting everything in.

keyTerms.def: The maximum number of tokens a model can pay attention to in a single call. Its temporary working memory, shared by input and output.
The small sub-word chunk a model reads and writes. Context windows are measured in tokens, not words or characters.
Everything you send into the window for one call — system instructions, documents, and conversation history combined.
A setting that reserves space in the window for the model's reply, so the answer isn't cut off when the prompt is large.
The tendency of models to use the start and end of a long context well, while skimming or ignoring material buried in the middle.
A running store of past tokens the model keeps in GPU memory during generation. It grows with the window, which is part of why long contexts are memory-hungry.
Classic attention's cost grows with the square of the token count: double the context and the work roughly quadruples. A key reason windows aren't infinite.
The earlier messages an app re-sends each turn so the model appears to remember. Once it overflows the window, the oldest turns are dropped.

quiz.q: A model has an 8,000-token context window. Your prompt (system instructions + pasted docs + chat history) already uses 7,800 tokens. What happens?
options: The model automatically expands the window to fit a full reply
Only about 200 tokens remain for the reply, so it will likely get cut off
The prompt is rejected because it must stay under half the window
The model deletes your oldest docs to make room for any reply length
quiz.explain: The window is shared by input and output. With 7,800 of 8,000 used, only ~200 tokens are left for the answer, so it gets truncated. Reserve room with max_tokens.

quiz.q: A user comes back the next day and the chatbot 'remembers' yesterday's conversation. How does this actually work?
options: The model permanently stored the chat in its weights overnight
The model has a hidden long-term memory that survives between calls
The app re-sends the earlier messages into the window each new turn
The context window never resets once a conversation has started
quiz.explain: Models have no memory between calls. The app re-pastes prior messages into the fresh window every turn; if that history overflows, the oldest turns are dropped.

quiz.q: You paste a 300-page document into a model with a huge window and it ignores a key fact from the middle. What best explains this?
options: The 'lost in the middle' effect — models attend best to the start and end
The document exceeded the token limit and was silently rejected
Long documents are always summarized before the model sees them
The fact was stored in training memory, not the context window
quiz.explain: Even within a large window, models reliably use the beginning and end but skim the middle. A big window lets you include more, but doesn't guarantee careful reading of all of it.

quiz.q: Your support bot must answer from a 500-page manual that's far too big to paste into every call. What is the standard fix?
options: Fine-tune the model on the manual so it never needs the context
Lower max_tokens until the whole manual fits in the prompt
Use RAG to retrieve only the few relevant passages per question
Split the manual across several models and merge their replies
quiz.explain: RAG fetches just the handful of relevant passages and places those in the window — cheaper, faster, and more reliable than brute-force pasting the whole manual.
